subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given curve is y=54x5-135x4-70x3+180x2+210x

Normal lines on this curve, are parallel to x+90y+2=0, hence slope of normal is m=-190, so slope of tangent to the given curve is -1m=90.

Slope of tangent on the given curve is

dydx=270x4-540x3-210x2+360x+210

Here, dydx=90   

⇒270x4-540x3-210x2+360x+210=90

⇒270x4-540x3-210x2+360x+120=0

⇒27x4-54x3-21x2+36x+12=0

⇒9x2-18x3-7x2+12x+4=0

⇒(x-1)(9x3-9x2-16x-4)=0

⇒(x-1)(x-2)(9x2+9x+2)=0

⇒(x-1)(x-2)(3x+2)(3x+1)=0

⇒x=1, 2, -23, -13

Hence, there are four points.

solution:
Given:

fx=x3-6x2+ax+b

Given that:

f2=0⇒2a+b=16    ....i

f4=0⇒4a+b=32    ....ii

Solving both equations, we get

a=8, b=0

∴  fx=x3-6x2+8x

⇒  fx=xx-2x-4

Now,

f'x=3x2-12x+8

Now,

If f'x=-1

⇒3x2-12x+8=-1

⇒3x2-12x+9=0

⇒x2-4x+3=0

⇒x2-3x-x+3=0

⇒x-1x-3=0

⇒x=1, 3

Since, x1∈2,4, therefore x1=3.

And,

f'x=0

⇒3x2-12x+8=0

⇒x=12±144-966

⇒x=12±486

⇒x=12±436=6±233

⇒x=2+233, 2-233

Then, 2+233∈2,4

Hence, x2=2+233

So, S1 is true.

Now,

f'x=x-2+233x-2-233

Sign scheme for f'x is as follows:

f'x>0 ∀x∈-∞,2-233∪2+233,∞, hence it is increasing.

f'x<0 ∀x∈2-233,2+233, hence it is decreasing.

So, x4=2+233.

Now,

2f'x3=3fx4

⇒23x32-12x3+8=32+2332+233-22+233-4

⇒23x32-12x3+8=3233+2233233-2

⇒23x32-12x3+8=2129-4

⇒3x32-12x3+8=-83

⇒9x32-36x3+32=0

⇒x3=36±1296-115218=36±1218

⇒x3=83, 43

So, S2 is true.

solution:
fx=ax5+bx4+cx3

Consider, limx→0⁡2+ax5+bx4+cx3x3=4

Differentiating fx with respect to x

f'x=5ax4+4bx3+6x2

=x25ax2+4bx+6

Critical points of the function are given as ±1.

f'1=0⇒5a+4b+6=0

So, fx=-65x5+2x3
And f'x=-6x4+6x2

=-6x2x+1x-1

subject: Mathematics
solution:
For $t < 0$, analyze $f(t) = \frac{|t+1|}{t^2}$ in two regions.
For $-1 < t < 0$:
$f(t) = \frac{t+1}{t^2}$, so $f'(t) = \frac{-(t+2)}{t^3} > 0$ (increasing).
For $t < -1$:
$f(t) = \frac{-t-1}{t^2}$, so $f'(t) = \frac{t+2}{t^3}$.
For $-2 < t < -1$:
$f'(t) < 0$ (decreasing).
For $t < -2$:
$f'(t) > 0$ (increasing).
Largest decreasing interval is $(-2, -1) = (2\alpha, \alpha)$, so $\alpha = -1$.
For $g(x) = 2\ln(x-2) - x^2 + 4x + 1$:
$g'(x) = \frac{2}{x-2} - 2x + 4 = 0$ gives $(x-2)^2 = 1$, so $x = 3$.
Since $g''(x) = -\frac{2}{(x-2)^2} - 2 < 0$, $x=3$ is maximum.
$g(3) = 2\ln(1) - 9 + 12 + 1 = 4$

$\Rightarrow f(x)=(x-1)^3$

solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Area }=(4 \cos \theta+2 \sin \theta)(2 \cos \theta+4 \sin \theta) \\ & =8 \cos ^2 \theta+16 \sin \theta \cos \theta+4 \sin \theta \cos \theta+8 \sin ^2 \theta \\ & =8+20 \sin \theta \cos \theta \\ & =8+10 \sin 2 \theta \\ & \text { Max Area }=8+10=18(\sin 2 \theta=1) \theta=45^{\circ} \\ & (a+b)^2=(4 \cos \theta+2 \sin \theta+2 \cos \theta+4 \sin \theta)^2 \\ & =(6 \cos \theta+6 \sin \theta)^2 \\ & =36(\sin \theta+\cos \theta)^2 \\ & =36(\sqrt{2})^2\end{aligned}$ $=72$

solution: Let $f^{\prime}(x)=a x^2+b x+c \Rightarrow f(x)=\frac{a x^3}{3}+\frac{b x^2}{2}+c x+d$ $\Rightarrow f(x)=\frac{1}{6}\left(2 a x^3+3 b x^2+6 c x+6 d\right)$, Now $f(1)=f(0)=d$, then according to Rolle's theorem $\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=a x^2+b x+c=0$ has at least one root in $(0,1)$

solution:
Given f0=0, f1=1 and f2=2

Let, hx=fx-x

Clearly hx, will be continuous and twice differentiable on 0, 2.

Now, h0=h1=h2=0

By Rolle's mean value theorem in 0, 1, we get

h'c1=0   

⇒f'c1-1=0   

⇒f'c1=1, where c1∈0, 1

Also, in the interval 1, 2

h'c2=0

⇒f׀c2-1=0

⇒f'c2=1, where c2∈1, 2

Now, use Rolle's theorem on c1, c2 for h'x, 

 We have, h''(c)=0

⇒f''(c)=0, where c∈c1, c2

Hence,  f''(c)=0 for some x∈0, 2.

Curve y=5x2+2x-25

Now tangent to curve at P will be,

y+1=22x-2

⇒y=22x-45

Now the tangent is also tangent to the curve y=x3-x2+x and passing through a,b so plottnig the diagram we get,

Now differentiating the curve y=x3-x2+x we get,

dydx=3x2-2x+1 

dydxQ=a,b=3a2-2a+1

So, equating with tangent slope we get,

3a2-2a+1=22

⇒3a2-2a-21=0

⇒3a2-9a+7a-21=0

⇒3a+7a-3=0

Now from curve b=a3-a2+a

So, at a=3 ⇒b=21

Also a=3 & b=21 are satisfying the line y=22x-45

So, 2a+9b=2×3+9×21=195

Note at a=-7/3 tangent will be parallel so it is rejected

solution:
Equation of normal is $Y-y=-\frac{d x}{d y}(X-x)$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow G \equiv\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}, 0\right) \\
& \left|x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right|=|2 x| \\
& \Rightarrow y \frac{d y}{d x}=x \text { or } y \frac{d y}{d x}=-3 x \\
& y d y=x d x \text { or } y d y=-3 x d x \\
& \frac{y^2}{2}=\frac{x^2}{2}+c \text { or } \frac{y^2}{2}=-\frac{3 x^2}{2}+c \\
& x^2-y^2=-2 c \text { or } 3 x^2+y^2=2 c.
\end{aligned}$

Applying Lagrange's mean value theorem
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{f(6)-f(1)}{5}=f^{\prime}(c) \geq 2 \\
& \Rightarrow f(6) \geq 10+f(1) \\
& \Rightarrow f(6) \geq 10-2 \\
& \Rightarrow f(6) \geq 8 .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
options: it is at a constant distance from the origin
solution:
Clearly $\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow$ slope of normal $=-\cot \theta$
Equation of normal at ' $\theta$ ' is
$y-a(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta)=-\cot \theta(x-a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta)$
$\Rightarrow y \sin \theta-a \sin ^2 \theta+a \theta \cos \theta \sin \theta=-x \cos \theta+a \cos ^2 \theta+a \theta \sin \theta \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow x \cos \theta+y \sin \theta=a$
Clearly this is an equation of straight line which is at a constant distance 'a' from origin.

solution:
Given y=fx=x3-x2-2x

⇒f1=1-1-2=-2

⇒f-1=-1-1+2=0

The slope of a line joining the points x1, y1 & x2, y2 is y2-y1x2-x1

Thus, the slope of the line segment joining the points 1, f1 & -1, f-1 is m=f(1)-f-11+1=-2-02=-1

Given, this line segment is parallel to the tangent of the curve y=fx, at x, y and we know that the slope of the tangent to y=fx is dydx, also the slope of two parallel lines is same, hence 

m=dydx=3x2-2x-2

⇒3x2-2x-2=-1

⇒3x2-2x-1=0

⇒x-13x+1=0

⇒x=1, -13.

Hence, the set S is -13, 1.

$f^{\prime \prime}(-1), f^{\prime \prime}(2)$ are negative. $f$ has local maxima at $-1,2$
